attack_surface.exposure: Resolve<T> walks PLangContext.Parent chain to find settings values. Cast<T> performs type conversion via Convert.ChangeType and Enum.ToObject.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Settings values originate from .pr file actions (trusted) or engine defaults (trusted). No external/untrusted input enters via this path.
mitigations: Cast<T> catches all conversion exceptions and returns class default
Case-insensitive key matching prevents shadow attacks
Goal scoping (save/null/restore) isolates settings per goal
gaps: Parent chain traversal has no depth limit (mitigated by CallStack.MaxDepth=1000)
Scope dictionary has no max size

attack_surface.exposure: Set() writes to context.SettingsScope (goal-local) or engine.Settings.Defaults (persistent). SettingsScope is public settable on PLangContext.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Only reachable from action handlers, which are dispatched from .pr files (trusted environment).
mitigations: Set(null) removes the key rather than storing null
isDefault flag separates persistent vs goal-scoped writes
gaps: No validation that stored values match the expected type for the key

attack_surface.exposure: archive.Settings.Max controls the gzip bomb protection limit (MaxDecompressedSize). A PLang goal can set this to 0 or long.MaxValue.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: User-sovereign: the user controls their own .pr files. Setting Max to 0 is equivalent to calling Environment.Exit() — the user's prerogative.
mitigations: Goal-scoped: a rogue setting in one goal doesn't persist to others (unless isDefault=true)
gaps: No minimum value enforcement — Max can be set to 0 (disabling decompression) or negative (undefined behavior in size comparison)

attack_surface.exposure: ModuleView<T> extracts module prefix from namespace via string manipulation (LastIndexOf('.')). For<T> creates a new instance per call.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Module prefix derived from compile-time namespace, not user input.
mitigations: Prefix comes from typeof(T).Namespace — attacker cannot control this at runtime
New instance per call prevents cross-context leakage

findings.vector: Scope dictionary (ConcurrentDictionary) has no maximum entry count. A PLang goal in a loop could call settings.set repeatedly with unique keys, growing the dictionary unboundedly.
findings.preconditions: User writes a PLang goal that sets many unique settings keys in a loop. Requires user-authored .pr file.
findings.impact: Memory exhaustion over time. Goal-scoped settings are cleared on goal exit, so this only persists if isDefault=true.
findings.exploit_sketch: foreach %items%, call SetUniqueSetting item=%item% — where each item generates a new key like 'archive.key_{item}'
findings.proposed_fix: Add a max entry count (e.g., 1000) to Scope. Log a warning when exceeded. Low priority since this is user-authored code.

findings.vector: Resolve<T> walks the PLangContext.Parent chain with no depth counter. If contexts nest deeply (goal calls goal calls goal...), the while loop traverses the entire chain on every settings lookup.
findings.preconditions: Deeply nested goal calls (up to CallStack.MaxDepth=1000). Each goal lookup walks the full chain.
findings.impact: O(n) traversal per settings read where n = nesting depth. At MaxDepth=1000 this is negligible. Not a stack overflow — it's iterative (while loop), not recursive.
findings.exploit_sketch: N/A — mitigated by CallStack depth limit. The while loop is iterative, not recursive, so no stack overflow risk.
findings.proposed_fix: No fix needed. The iterative while loop is safe even at depth 1000. Document the O(depth) complexity.

findings.vector: Cast<T> swallows all exceptions with a bare catch. If Convert.ChangeType throws an unexpected exception (OutOfMemoryException, ThreadAbortException), it would be silently swallowed.
findings.preconditions: Extremely unusual runtime conditions (OOM during type conversion).
findings.impact: Silent failure — returns class default instead of propagating a critical exception. In practice, Convert.ChangeType only throws FormatException, InvalidCastException, OverflowException.
findings.proposed_fix: Narrow the catch to catch (Exception) and re-throw critical exceptions (OutOfMemoryException, StackOverflowException). Very low priority.

findings.vector: archive.Settings.Max can be set to 0 or negative values, effectively disabling gzip bomb protection or causing undefined behavior in size comparisons.
findings.preconditions: User writes a PLang goal that sets max gzip size to 0 or -1.
findings.impact: By threat model, this is user-sovereign behavior. The user controls their own .pr files. However, if a future transport-received Data envelope carries settings, this could become an attack vector.
findings.exploit_sketch: set max gzip size to 0 — then decompress a 10GB payload
findings.proposed_fix: Add a minimum floor (e.g., 1MB) in the Decompress method: Math.Max(settings.Max, MIN_DECOMPRESSED_SIZE). Only matters if settings become settable from untrusted sources.

summary: The Settings infrastructure is well-designed and introduces minimal new attack surface. All settings originate from .pr files (trusted) or engine defaults (trusted). The scope chain properly isolates goal-level settings and restores state on exit. Thread safety is handled via ConcurrentDictionary. Type conversion is defensive with silent fallback to defaults. No critical or high severity findings. The 4 low/medium findings are all accepted-risk under the user-sovereign threat model. The most important future consideration is: if settings ever become writable from untrusted sources (e.g., transport-received Data), the lack of value validation (finding #4) and unbounded dictionary growth (finding #1) would need to be addressed.
